turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has the opinion this morning in Case 13-628, Zivotofsky v. Kerry.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: The petitioner here Zivotofsky was born in Jerusalem to parents who are United States citizens, so that makes him a United States citizen as well. His parents requested a United States passport and a Consular Report of Birth Abroad for their son that would list his birthplace as Israel, but that posed the problem. The President and the Secretary of State, consistent with the policy of their predecessors, do not recognize Israel or any other country as sovereign over Jerusalem. As a result the policy of the Department of State is that Zivotofsky's passport must show his place of birth as Jerusalem. The policy does not allow the Secretary to list the place of birth as Israel. Zivotofsky's parents sued on his behalf. They claimed that a Congressional statute entitled Zivotofsky to have Israel listed on the passport. And the statute is the Foreign Relations Authorization Act for the fiscal year of 2003 and the key section is §214(d) of that Act. That section requires the Secretary of State to list Israel as the place of birth for anyone who was born in Jerusalem who requests, who requests, that designation. The President however has refused to enforce this Congressional enactment. The President's position is that the statute infringes on his exclusive power to recognize foreign sovereigns. The Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit agreed with the President. It held that §214(d) unconstitutionally infringes on an exclusive executive power. This Court granted certiorari and it now addresses two questions to resolve the interbranch dispute between Congress and the executive. First, does the President have the exclusive power to grant formal recognition to a foreign sovereign? Second, if he has that power, can Congress command him to issue a passport designation, a formal statement, that contradicts his recognition determination? This Court now holds first the President does have the exclusive power to recognize foreign sovereigns. Second, Congress by this statute cannot command him to contradict that recognition determination. It is axiomatic that when the President takes measures incompatible with the will of Congress, he can rely only upon his own constitutional powers, minus any constitutional powers of Congress over the matter. So for the President to prevail here, his asserted power must be both exclusive and conclusive so that Congress cannot act on this subject. Turning to the question whether the power to recognize in a formal sense the legitimacy of other states and governments, including their territorial bounds, is exclusive or shared, the Court now holds that the power is exclusively in the President. This holding is based on the Constitution's text and structure, as well as precedent and history bearing on the question. As a matter of constitutional structure, the President's power is to receive ambassadors, make treaties, and appoint ambassadors, give him control over recognition decisions. The President alone is given the duty to receive ambassadors. This constitutes recognition of the sovereignty of the sending state. In addition, recognition may concur on the conclusion of a bilateral treaty or the formal initiation of diplomatic relations, including the dispatch of an ambassador. Each of these acts requires presidential consent. Recognition is a matter on which the nation must speak with one voice. That voice must be the President's. As a structural and functional matter, the power to recognize foreign sovereigns is the President's alone. The Court's cases support this conclusion. Today's opinion discusses the precedents on which the Court's holding rest. The Court also sets forward the history in this area, which does give support to the conclusion that the President's power is exclusive. The weight of historical evidence indicates that Congress has accepted that the power to recognize foreign states and governments and their territorial balance belongs to the presidency. The Secretary urges the Court to go beyond the recognition power. He submits that the Court should set forth a broad statement of presidential powers over foreign affairs based on its earlier opinions in -- opinion in United States v. Curtiss-Wright Export Company -- Corporation. But for the reasons the Court gives, the Curtiss-Wright case does not extend so far as the Secretary suggests. In a world that is ever more compressed and interdependent, it is essential that the congressional role in foreign affairs be understood and respected. It is not for the President alone to determine the whole content of the nation's foreign policy. Still, since the power to recognize foreign states does reside in the President alone, the question then becomes whether §214 infringes on the executive's recognition power. The Court holds that it does infringe on this executive power. This conclusion is a matter of both commonsense and necessity. If Congress could command the President to state a recognition position inconsistent with his own, as §214(d) requires, Congress could override the President's recognition determination. That is so because as a matter of international law recognition may be affected by a mere declaration on the part of the recognizing state. In holding §214(d) invalid, the Court does not question the substantial powers of Congress over foreign affairs in general or passports in particular. This case is confined solely to the exclusive power of the President to control recognition determinations, including formal statements by the executive branch acknowledging the legitimacy of a state or government and its territorial bounds. For these reasons and others set forth in the opinion, the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit is affirmed. Justice Breyer has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment in part and dissenting in part. The Chief Justice has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Alito joins. Justice Scalia has filed a dissenting opinion in which the Chief Justice and Justice Alito join.
